summary: Updated the repository .clang-format include-category priorities so clang-format places a C file's corresponding main header first, e.g. fighter.c including fighter.h before all other includes. The rationale was to make each main header self-sufficient: if the corresponding .h is included first in its .c file, missing standalone dependencies are exposed instead of being hidden by earlier transitive includes.
what_changed: Kept IncludeBlocks: Regroup and the existing IncludeCategories regex ordering, but shifted category Priority values upward.
Moved prior negative and zero include-group priorities into positive ranges so clang-format's implicit main-include priority 0 remains first.

Examples from the diff: platform.h and placeholder.h Priority changed from -2 to 1; forward.h groups changed from -1 to 2; static headers changed from 0 to 3; local quoted includes changed from 1 to 4; catch-all quoted/path groups changed from 2 to 5; angle-bracket include groups changed from 3 to 6.
SortPriority values were preserved, maintaining the previous relative sorting order among non-main include groups.
smart_moves: Used clang-format's default main-header behavior instead of trying to encode per-file header names manually.
Preserved existing SortPriority values, so the fix was narrowly scoped to group placement rather than reshuffling all include ordering.
Avoided changing source files directly; updating the formatter configuration lets future formatting consistently enforce the intended include invariant.
The priority shift made room for clang-format's unconfigurable main-include priority 0 while keeping the old non-main group hierarchy intact.
decomp_lessons: For decomp C files, include the file's matching main header first to verify that the header can be included standalone.
A header that only works after other includes is fragile because its required types, macros, or declarations are being supplied transitively.
Formatting configuration can enforce useful correctness properties in addition to style.
When changing include ordering rules, preserve existing intra-group sort priorities if the goal is only to introduce a new first group.
naming_conventions: The PR describes the corresponding header as the "main" include: for a source file like fighter.c, the matching include is "fighter.h".

.clang-format include categories use regexes for special project header classes such as platform.h, placeholder.h, forward.h, *.static.h, quoted local headers, dolphin headers, sysdolphin/baselib headers, and generic angle-bracket headers.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: No direct assembly matching tactic was present.

Indirectly useful for matching work: making headers standalone can reveal missing declarations or type dependencies earlier, reducing include-order-sensitive build behavior while refactoring decomp units.
review_feedback: No review comments were present in the provided slice.
The PR body explicitly justified the change: the main include should be first because each .c file has a corresponding .h file, and first inclusion guarantees the header can be included standalone.
The PR body noted a clang-format constraint: the main include has priority 0 by default and this apparently cannot be changed, so other include groups were bumped to priorities above 0.
follow_up_queries: Find source files where the corresponding main header is not the first include after this formatter change.
Check whether any headers fail to compile standalone when included before all other dependencies.
Search for include-order-sensitive headers that rely on transitive includes rather than declaring or including their own dependencies.
Inspect future .clang-format changes for accidental disruption of main-header priority 0 behavior.
